coredata.dc:title: Surface-engineered anisotropic g-C3N4 photocatalysts via green-exfoliation for visible light-driven water remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Vijayarangan, R.
Mohan, Sakar
Selvaraj, Manickam
Assiri, Mohammed A.
Ilangovan, R.
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the modification of g-C3N4 (GCN) using extracts of betel (Piper betle) leaves and veld-grape (Cissus quadrangularis) stems. The obtained results indicate that these green extracts can effectively exfoliate the GCN-layers, modifying both the 2D structure and the 3D morphological features of GCN, leading to simultaneous chemical and physical modifications in the system. The FESEM and HRTEM images revealed the exfoliated layers with a spike-like morphology for betel extract-treated GCN (B-GCN) system, while a porous nature, along with curled layer structure, is observed in the veld extract-treated GCN (V-GCN) system. An extended absorption in visible-light region along with a reduced band gap energy is observed for V-GCN (∼2.22 eV) and B-GCN (∼2.29 eV), compared to pristine GCN (∼2.41 eV). These improvements attributed to the anisotropic morphology-induced alterations in the band structure of the systems. A notable carrier recombination resistance is also realized from the PL spectra of the treated-GCNs. The visible light-driven photocatalytic efficiencies of both the pristine and treated-GCNs in degrading methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB) and their mixture (MB+RhB) are investigated under solar irradiation. The results revealed that the V-GCN and B-GCN exhibited around 100% and over 80% degradation for MB and RhB dye, respectively. Notably, in the case of mixed dyes, the treated-GCN systems demonstrated enhanced degradation compared to pristine. This enhancement can be attributed to their improved band structure resulting from a greater layer exfoliation, morphological changes, and the presence of nitrogen defects in the modified systems. The recycling studies demonstrated that the extract-treated GCN is sable and can remain sustainable for scaled-up photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
2D structures
Exfoliation
Green synthesis
Defects
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Khalid University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Group Research Project under grant number R.G.P. 2/354/44.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation of green extracts of betel leaves and veld grape stem, and the photographs of the pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 powders.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 systems.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1 s and (b) N 1 s in pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 systems.
(a)-(c) FESEM images and (d)-(f) HRTEM images of pristine, betel leaves- and veld stems extracts-treated g-C3N4 systems, respectively.
(a) BET N2 sorption isotherms, (b) UV-visible absorption spectra, (c) Tauc's plots for band gap estimation and (d) PL spectra of the pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 systems.

C/C0 degradation plots of (a) methylene blue, (b) rhodamine B, (c) MB+RhB mixed dye, and (d) percentage of TOC presence in the respective dye solutions at the end of 2 h degraded by pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 systems.
(a) Radical scavenging during the degradation of MB+RhB dye mixture by V-GCN system and (b) photocatalytic mechanism of pristine & extract-treated g-C3N4 photocatalysts.
(a) Photocurrent density and (b) electrochemical impedance measurements of the pristine and extract-treated g-C3N4 systems.
(a) Cyclic efficiency of V-GCN towards photocatalytic degradation of MB+RhB dye mixture, (b) XRD pattern, (c) UV–Visible absorption spectrum and (d) FESEM image of the recycled V-GCN photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Surface-engineered anisotropic g-C3N4 photocatalysts via green-exfoliation for visible light-driven water remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600025, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aNational Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600025, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nano and Material Sciences, Jain (Deemed-to-be University), Jain Global Campus, Kanakapura, Bangalore, Karnataka 562112, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Nano and Material Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCentre for Nano and Material Sciences, Jain (Deemed-to-be University), Jain Global Campus, Kanakapura, Bangalore, Karnataka 562112, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 61413, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Khalid University, Abha 61413, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the modification of g-C3N4 (GCN) using extracts of betel (Piper betle) leaves and veld-grape (Cissus quadrangularis) stems. The obtained results indicate that these green extracts can effectively exfoliate the GCN-layers, modifying both the 2D structure and the 3D morphological features of GCN, leading to simultaneous chemical and physical modifications in the system. The FESEM and HRTEM images revealed the exfoliated layers with a spike-like morphology for betel extract-treated GCN (B-GCN) system, while a porous nature, along with curled layer structure, is observed in the veld extract-treated GCN (V-GCN) system. An extended absorption in visible-light region along with a reduced band gap energy is observed for V-GCN (∼2.22 eV) and B-GCN (∼2.29 eV), compared to pristine GCN (∼2.41 eV). These improvements attributed to the anisotropic morphology-induced alterations in the band structure of the systems. A notable carrier recombination resistance is also realized from the PL spectra of the treated-GCNs. The visible light-driven photocatalytic efficiencies of both the pristine and treated-GCNs in degrading methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB) and their mixture (MB+RhB) are investigated under solar irradiation. The results revealed that the V-GCN and B-GCN exhibited around 100% and over 80% degradation for MB and RhB dye, respectively. Notably, in the case of mixed dyes, the treated-GCN systems demonstrated enhanced degradation compared to pristine. This enhancement can be attributed to their improved band structure resulting from a greater layer exfoliation, morphological changes, and the presence of nitrogen defects in the modified systems. The recycling studies demonstrated that the extract-treated GCN is sable and can remain sustainable for scaled-up photocatalytic applications.

Manifestation of green-extract mediated exfoliation of g-C3N4 into layered-nanostructures for the improved photocatalytic simultaneous degradation of multiple dyes under solar-irradiation.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well established that the photocatalysis is a potential process, which shows a wide range of applications in environmental remediation and energy generation [1]. Among the materials developed for photocatalysis, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has gained a significant importance due to its narrow band gap energy, 2D layer structure, tunable physiochemical properties, and facile synthesis requirements [2–4]. Notably, the narrow band gap energy of g-C3N4 (GCN) enables it to be activated under visible light irradiation [5]. Its suitable band edge positions manifest multiple photocatalytic processes, including pollutant degradation, water splitting, CO2 reduction, and N2 fixation [6–8]. However, GCN requires further improvements in terms of charge carrier separation, carrier transfer to the surface, recombination resistance, and quantum efficiency [9–11]. Consequently, various modifications have been explored to improve the properties of GCN, including doping [12,13], defect engineering [9,14], and composite formation [15]. Among these strategies, intrinsic modifications through surface modifications [16,17] surface functionalization [18], exfoliation into thin layers [17] emerge as fundamental and effective approaches to enhance the properties of GCN. Such modifications are crucial for a deeper understanding of GCN in its pristine form and provide a foundation for further enhancements.

Various means of surface modifications include anchoring functional groups on the surface [19], creating surface defects, making surface textures, tuning surface potential, and more [20,21]. Among these, the surface modifications achieved by attaching functional groups could be more influential compared to other methods. The attachment of functional groups provides more reactive sites, making the material more receptive and sensitive to external stimuli [22,23] The improvements through these modifications enhance the overall properties of the system. Strategies for integrating functional groups involve treating the GCN surface with various mild to harsh chemicals, such as amines, hydrogen peroxide, potassium permanganate, sulfuric acid, etc. [24–26]. However, using similar functional groups present in the green molecules can be more environmentally benign, biocompatible, nontoxic, biodegradable, and versatile for the desired applications [26,27]. As an efficient material, GCN is a well-established photocatalyst, and treating it with biomolecules such as green extracts could equip it with more functional groups, leading to exfoliation into thin layers and modification of their surfaces. This makes them to be more conducive to surface adsorption of surrounding molecules, improving redox and other associated catalytic processes [28,29]. Both the exfoliation and surface modification in GCN improves their surface area by inducing functional groups and chemical moieties on the surface. This eventually provides more active sites for rapid interfacial interactions and improves the selective adsorption of surrounding species, essential for catalytic reactions. It is also widely observed that the thin-layer GCN systems exhibit improved light absorption, charge carrier separation, transfer, and recombination resistance during the photocatalytic reactions [20]. The origin of such improved properties can be attributed to the 2D quantum confinement effects in GCN leading to the formation of discrete energy band structure influencing both the optical and electronic properties of GCN [26,27]. Notably, the modifications in GCN also improve their dispersion ability in solvents, facilitating their effective interactions with surroundings and receptive to the external stimuli such as heat, light and pressure, enabling them for versatile applications. Surface modifications also improve the stability of GCN, emerging from their improved mechanical and regenerative chemical surface due to their altered interlayer interactions in the system.

In this context, herein, we have synthesized GCN and treated with betel (Piper betle) leaves and veld grape (Cissus quadrangularis) stem extract to modify the surface of GCN. While these extracts have previously been utilized in the synthesis of metal and metal oxide systems [30–32], this study marks the first application of these extracts in the modification of GCN. Existing studies predominantly characterize the green extracts for their chemical reduction properties towards the synthesis of materials, known as green synthesis process. In contrast, the current study demonstrates the potential of these green extracts for exfoliation and surface modifications process, which is an approach seldom explored in the literature [33]. The controlled preparation of extracts, as demonstrated in this study, preserves the phytochemicals in the extracts, facilitating their involvement in reactions that leads to the exfoliation and modification of the GCN surface. The phytochemicals present in these extracts are rich in functional groups, including hydroxyl and carboxylic groups. Consequently, these compounds induce both chemical and physical modifications in GCN, with the former involving the anchoring of functional groups on the surface and the latter involving the exfoliation of GCN into thin layers, along with the creation of porous and curled structures of GCN layers. In this context, this study investigates the mechanism of green extracts in the modification of GCN and the structure-property relationship of these modified GCN systems for photocatalytic applications. For instance, while not all extracts are necessarily capable of reducing metal precursors to form nanomaterials as in a bottom-up approach, they can also be potentially used for the post-modification of synthesized materials, enabling the formation nanostructures akin to a top-down approach. Furthermore, in their photocatalytic applications, the developed pristine and extract-treated GCN systems are employed for the degradation of a solution containing single and multiple dyes, which simulates the industrial wastewater. Notably, degradation of mixed dyes is also seldom explored in the literature [26]. Overall, a systematic study was conducted in order to understand the underlying mechanism of the green modification of GCN and the design features of modified GCN towards photocatalytic applications.

In the typical synthesis of g-C3N4, 4 g of melamine was taken in an alumina crucible and annealed at 550 °C for 3 h. The obtained g-C3N4 solids were crushed to fine powders using mortar-pestle and stored at room temperature for further use [34,35].

Betel leaves and veld grape stems were collected from random locations where the plants naturally thriving in Thanjavur, Tamil Nadu, India (geographical coordinates: latitude 10.786999, longitude 79.137825) during March-April. For the preparation of betel leaves extract, certain quantity of leaves was taken, washed with fresh tap water, and air-dried in the shade for around 3 days. Subsequently, ∼100 g of dried leaves was taken in a 250 mL round-bottom flask containing a mixture of 80 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of double-distilled water. Then, this mixture was refluxed for about 12 h at 100 °C using an oil bath setup. The resulting refluxed solution was then filtered using a filter paper to obtain the final betel leaves extract. Similarly, certain amount of veld grape stems was taken, washed, and dried using the same procedure as described above. A same procedure was repeated by taking ∼100 g of dried veld grape stems to obtain its extract. After the preparation, both solutions were stored in air-tight glass vials at room temperature for further use. The FTIR spectra of the prepared betel leaves and veld grape stem extracts were obtained to analyze the various functional groups present in the extracts. The spectra and discussions are provided in Fig. S1 in the supporting file. The extract was prepared in triplicate to ensure consistency, confirmed through FTIR analysis, and then utilized for comprehensive studies.

In the extract-treatment process, 50 mL of the prepared green extract (betel leaves/veld grape stem extract) was taken in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. The pH of the betel leaves and veld grape stem extract was measured to be around 6.2 and 5.7, respectively. Subsequently, around 1 g of synthesized g-C3N4 powder was added to the reaction flask and sonicated for 30–45 min to achieve homogeneous dispersion of the powder in the extract. The mixture was then refluxed at 80 °C for 4 h using an oil bath under stirring condition. Afterward, the solution-mixture was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, and the settled powder was collected through vacuum filtration and dried at 60 °C overnight.

The obtained extract-treated g-C3N4 powders, named B-GCN and V-GCN for betel leaves and veld grape stem extracts, respectively, were stored in glass vials at room temperature for further studies. Schematic representation of the synthesis processes and photographs of the samples are depicted in Fig. 1 .

A mixed dye solution was prepared containing 10 ppm of methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB) to partially simulate a model industrial pollutant. In addition to this, separate 10 ppm solutions of MB and RhB were prepared for comparative degradation studies of each dye with respect to their mixtures. In the photocatalytic degradation experiments, 50 mg of the synthesized photocatalyst was added to 100 mL of the dye solution and stirred magnetically in the dark condition to ensure to attainment of sorption equilibrium in the system. Subsequently, this reaction solution was exposed to direct natural solar irradiation. The photocatalytic experiments were conducted in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India (geographical coordinates: latitude 13.067439, longitude 80.237617) from March to May. The sunlight intensity during the experiments was measured to be around 120,000 lux using a lux meter. All experiments were carried out between 10:30 and 14:00 in sunny days. Dye degradation was monitored by measuring its optical density (at 665 and 554 nm for MB and RhB, respectively) as a function of time using a UV–visible absorption spectrometer. Optical density of the dyes was recorded every 30 min, and C/C0 plots were drawn to estimate the degradation percentage of the dyes.

Structural and functional group analyses were carried out using X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Mini Flux XRD) and Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS50FT-IR). Chemical/electronic state analyses of the samples were performed using the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ULVAC-PHI Inc, PHI5000-III). Morphology analysis was carried using field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7100F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Techni-G2-S TWIN, FEI). Optical properties were analyzed using UV–Visible absorption (PerkinElmer Lambda 650) and photoluminescence spectrometer (FluoroMax Plus, Horiba). The surface area and surface characteristics of the samples were analyzed using a BET (Brunauer-Emmet-Teller) N2 sorption measurements on a BET BELSORP Max equipment.

The preparation of electrodes for electrochemical impedance measurements involved a typical three-electrode setup with platinum-wire, catalyst-coated FTO glass, and Ag/AgCl solution as a counter-, working-, and reference- electrode, respectively. The FTO working electrode was prepared by cleaning it using ultrasonication in water-ethanol mixture bath for one hour and then washed with acetone and dried at 60 °C in vacuum oven. The active area of FTO glass was 1 × 1 cm2. A thick-dispersion consisting of ∼10 mg of photocatalyst, 3 mL methanol and a small amount of 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (binding agent) was prepared and drop-casted on a FTO substrate. These fabricated electrodes were investigated for their photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance using an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT204). For photocurrent measurements, the electrodes were operated with an applied bias of 0.8 V in the presence 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as electrolyte and the system was irradiated under a 150 W Xe lamp with 10 s on-off cycles.

The XRD patterns of pristine g-C3N4 (P-GCN), and g-C3N4 treated with the extracts of betel leaves and veld grape stems (B-GCN and V-GCN, respectively) are shown in Fig. 2 (a). Notably, the 2θ peak at ∼12.9° observed for P-GCN [36] disappeared in B-GCN and is deformed in V-GCN. Likewise, the 2θ peak at ∼27.4° is slightly shifted and broadened for both treated-GCN systems compared to pristine-GCN [27,34,37]. These observations indicate a significant influence of the green treatment process on the 2D structure of GCN layers, particularly in terms of their in-planer structure and layer stacking. Among the treated samples, the 27.4° peak of V-GCN is considerably broadened and shifted to higher 2θ angle compared to B-GCN [37]. These shifts in the diffraction peaks and their broadening can be attributed to defects in GCN layers and layer thinning, respectively. In this perspective, both changes are observed in V-GCN, confirming that the veld extract robustly modifies the GCN layers due to greater exfoliation along with surface and bulk morphology modifications in the system.

The presence of functional groups in the synthesized materials was analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy and the obtained spectra are displayed in Fig. 2(b). The intensity of the IR band corresponding to the triazine unit is found to be influenced in the case of B-GCN and V-GCN compared to P-GCN. This suggests that the triazine ring is considerably modified due to the green treatment, and it could be associated with the presence of defects in the system. Similarly, the P-GCN shows the IR bands corresponding to C = O, C = N, and CN stretching [38], and these bands are greatly influenced in the case of the extract-treated GCN systems. Additionally, a CH stretching band is observed for both B-GCN and V-GCN, which suggests that the functional groups in the green extracts actively interact with GCN and modify them (Fig. S1). Furthermore, IR bands corresponding to NH and OH stretching are prominently shifted and broadened in the case of both B-GCN and V-GCN, suggesting a considerable change in the surface characteristics of the materials. Notably, these functional groups often act as bridging network, establishing interactive connections between the surface and surrounding molecules, facilitating the materials to be more receptive and responsive to the external physiochemical stimuli [39].

The chemical states of elements (i.e., C and N) present in pristine and extract-treated GCN were determined using XPS, and the obtained spectra are displayed in Fig. 3 (a)-(b). The carbon-corrected deconvoluted carbon (C 1 s) spectra of the samples reveal signals corresponding to C = C, CNH2, and N = CN in the characteristic binding energy range at 284.6, 287.1, and 287.7 eV, respectively, for carbon in bulk GCN (Fig. 3(a)) [40]. However, these peaks are slightly shifted towards lower binding energy in extract-treated GCN, suggesting a considerable influence on the carbon network. This shift may be attributed to the attachments of host-carbon to the functional groups of biomolecules present in the green-extracts.

Notably, a deconvoluted peak corresponding to NC-O appeared in the case of extract-treated GCN, indicating an effective anchoring of green molecules on the material's surface. In general, the overall binding energy of carbon in V-GCN is considerably decreased (negative shifted) compared to B-GCN. This could be due to the acidic nature (H +) of veld grape facilitating a chemical etching of GCN, leading to surface-level chemical off-stoichiometry in the material, supporting effective interaction of surface-carbon with functional groups of the veld grape. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(b)) of pristine-GCN show typical peaks corresponding to graphene N, CN = C and NC3 system at their characteristics binding energies of 398.0, 399.8, and 401.8 eV, respectively [40,41]. These peaks in the N 1 s spectra of both B-GCN and V-GCN are slightly shifted, with an additional deconvoluted peak at 402.4 eV observed for V-GCN, corresponding to π-excitation. This suggests the presence of N-based vacancy defects in the V-GCN system [41]. These observations indicate that green-modifications chemically reduce the surface of GCN, affecting its surface features and leading to the formation of nitrogen defects in the system.

The morphological features of pristine and extract-treated GCN (B-GCN and V-GCN) systems were investigated using FESEM and HRTEM techniques, and the obtained images are displayed in Fig. 4 (a)-(f). The obtained images clearly illustrate significant exfoliation and modification of GCN layers in both cases of green treatments [21]. Interestingly, the GCN layers treated with betel extract (B-GCN) are exfoliated into multilayers coupled with the formation of spike-like structures on the surface. In contrast, the GCN layers treated with veld grape extract (V-GCN) exhibit a porous nature along with improved layer exfoliation into curled structures [27]. The origin of layer-curling in V-GCN could be attributed to the nature of their functional groups, which affect the triazine-skeleton through a shear force during the permeation of molecules into the layers. This process causes layer exfoliations, creating localized polar interactions that lead to the curvature and curling of the exfoliated GCN layers [29]. These observed stronger exfoliation of GCN layers due to the chemical treatments is supported by the disappearance and deformation of the peak at ∼12.9° in the respective XRD patterns.

The intercalation of molecules from veld-extract into the GCN layers causes exfoliation due to their deep interactions via chemical reduction reactions. This process leads to chemical etching and the creation of pores on the surface of GCN layers. The observed peak shift and broadening in the XRD patterns, along with the confirmation of N-vacancies via XPS N 1 s spectra, can be correlated with this proposed mechanism of pore creation in the V-GCN system. From these results, it is evident that the green-treatment induces both physical and chemical modifications in GCN through shallow and deep processes. For instance, the exfoliation and creation of pores on the surface of GCN layers can be considered as shallow and deep physical modifications, respectively. Similarly, the surface attachments of functional groups and the creation of defects in GCN layers can be regarded as shallow and deep chemical modifications induced by green-treatments.

The BET (Brunauer-Emmet-Teller) surface analysis of the samples was conducted using N2 adsorption-desorption measurements, and the obtained curves are displayed in Fig. 5 (a). From the curves, a surface area of ∼23.2, 36.9, and 55.5 m2/g was estimated for P-GCN, B-GCN, and V-GCN, respectively. All the samples exhibit a type III isotherm indicating the characteristics of a reversible mesoporous surface with an unrestricted multilayer structure enabling gradual absorption-desorption of adsorbates in the system [42]. However, hysteresis corresponding to type H3 is observed for B-GCN, and this hysteresis is further increased in the case of V-GCN. This feature indicates that the lateral interactions between the material and adsorbate are relatively stronger than their interactions on the surface, owing to the improved multilayer structures. This observation is in correlation with the FESEM images of V-GCN, which show aggregated, loosely-assembled plate-like structures with slit-like pores in the material. These results highlight that the green-treatment, especially with veld grape extract, not only modifies the surface (2D) but also the bulk (3D) structure of the material, leading to the modifications of materials in all spatial facets. This collective modification results in the creation of multilayers and complex porous structures, enhancing effective interactions with the surroundings and facilitating physiochemical reactions in all facets of the materials for favorable applications [43].

The UV–Visible absorption spectra of P-GCN, B-GCN, and V-GCN are presented in Fig. 5(b), revealing improved optical properties in the extract-treated GCN compared to P-GCN. This absorption edges are found to be shifted towards visible light region, attributed to the morphological changes and defects-induced energy level dispersion in the systems, particularly N-vacancies in V-GCN [44]. Notably, the absorption of V-GCN is enhanced in the wavelength range of 300–400 nm, suggesting the effective absorption of UV light photons in addition to visible light photons. Consistent with these observations, Tauc's plots were used to estimate band gap energy of 2.41, 2.29, and 2.22 eV for P-GCN, B-GCN and V-GCN, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5(c) [45,46]. The trends in the absorption profile of the materials and their estimated band gap energies suggest that the improved optical properties of the green-modified systems can be largely assigned to modifications in the band structures arising from both physical and chemical modifications of GCN systems. The absorption spectra of the extracts and discussions are provided in Fig. S2 in the supporting file.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pristine and extract-treated GCNs are displayed in Fig. 5(d), obtained under an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The spectra exhibit radiative emission peak at ∼441, 444 and 466 nm for P-GCN, B-GCN, and V-GCN, respectively. Notably, the peak profile of pristine-GCN is narrow, while it is relatively broadened with decreased intensity for both the extract-treated GCN systems. Additionally, a hump-like peak is observed for P-GCN, B-GCN, and V-GCN at ∼460, 474 and 493 nm, respectively, which could be attributed to the emissions from defect levels and modulated band structures in the systems. The reduced PL intensity suggests that the charge recombination in treated-GCN is relatively slow compared to pristine GCN, which is beneficial for photocatalytic process [45]. Importantly, a shift in the peaks corresponding to secondary-emissions is observed in B-GCN and V-GCN could originate from the cascading recombination of charge carriers in the system. For instance, in the case of V-GCN, while the majority of electrons recombine with holes emitting the photons of 466 nm, some trapped electrons in the defect levels emit the photons of ∼493 nm upon recombination with holes [47]. The presence of both intrinsic and extrinsic emissions (due to exfoliation and defects) in these treated GCNs can be supportive to inhibit the charge recombination process in the system, contributing to their improved photocatalytic reactions.

The photocatalytic performances of the synthesized systems were evaluated based on their ability to degrade methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB) dye, and their mixture (MB+RhB) solution [34,48]. Following a pre-optimized condition of 100 mL of 10 ppm concentrated dye and 50 mg of photocatalyst, the dye degradation study was conducted under natural solar irradiation. The obtained C/C0 plots corresponding to the degradation of MB, RhB, and MB+RhB mixed dyes are displayed in Fig. 6 (a)-(c). The results indicate that the P-GCN, B-GCN, and V-GCN photocatalyst degraded ∼82, 93, and 100% of MB dye in 120 min, while it is ∼77, 91, and 100% in the case of RhB dye, respectively. In the case of mixed dye (MB+RhB) solution, the ability of pristine-GCN to degrade the RhB dye decreased significantly to around 59%, compared to ∼76% for MB dye, as shown in Fig. 6(c). In contrast, B-GCN exhibited degradation percentages of ∼93% for MB and 87% for RhB, while V-GCN showed complete degradation (∼100%) for MB and 98% for RhB.

Furthermore, the presence of total organic carbon (TOC), indicating the mineralization of dyes (MB, RhB, and MB+RhB mixture) in the respective degraded solutions was assessed, and the results are displayed in Fig. 6(d). The TOC presence in single-dye solutions (i.e., MB and RhB) degraded by the respective photocatalysts can be correlated with the degradation efficiency estimated using their respective C/C0 plots (Fig. 6(a)-(b)). However, the TOC percentage appears more pronounced in the MB+RhB mixed dye degraded solutions, representing the collective presence of TOC from both MB and RhB in the mixed solutions. For instance, V-GCN exhibited around 100% degradation of MB and RhB dye solutions at the end of 2 h, as shown in Fig. 6(a)-(b). Consequently, a trace presence of ∼1 and 1.2% of TOC is observed in the degraded solutions, indicating the presence of residual fragmented organic carbons in the respective solutions. In the case of mixed (MB+RhB) dye degradation, the C/C0 plot showed ∼100 and 93% degradation of the respective dyes (Fig. 6(c)). Consequently, a TOC of ∼7.9% is observed in the degraded solution, likely attributed to the presence of residual fragmented organic carbon from both dyes in the degraded solutions.

The observed enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of both B-GCN and V-GCN, in comparison with P-GCN, can be attributed to the improved physiochemical properties resulting from the green-treatments-induced modifications in the system, as confirmed by various characterization results. These decreased ability of pristine-GCN to degrade RhB in the mixed solution many be attributed to the polar interaction of MB and RhB dye, leading to incompatibility toward competing redox reactions and poor degradation of dyes in the mixture [34]. However, due to green-modifications, the surface of B-GCN and V-GCN systems become ionic-conducive and steric interactive toward the mixed dyes, facilitating their effective simultaneous degradation. In addition to these features, the improved band structures of B-GCN and V-GCN systems support favorable electronic and optical process, facilitating efficient charge carrier separation and delocalization in the materials during the photocatalytic reactions. This active participation of charge carriers in redox reactions leads to the effective degradation of dyes, even in the mixture.

Further, to identify the radicals responsible for driving the dye degradation process, scavenger molecules such as benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were introduced into the dye solution (MB+RhB mixture) during the photocatalytic reaction over the V-GCN system. The results (Fig. 7 (a)) indicate that the collective degradation percentages of the mixed dyes decreased to around 11, 47, and 69% in the presence of BQ, IPA and EDTA corresponding to the O2 ●
− , OH● radicals, and h + carriers. This suggests that the degradation is primarily driven by O2 ●
− radicals, generated during the photocatalytic reactions [49,50]. Based on this observation, a plausible photocatalytic mechanism of V-GCN is proposed as schematically depicted in Fig. 7(b).

Upon excitation under solar light, the electrons are excited to the conduction band (CB) creating holes in the valence band (VB) of the system. Owing to the dispersion of these energy bands, the charge carriers in the respective bands are quantized and effectively delocalized and promoted to the surface of the V-GCN layers. Meanwhile, due to the surface modifications, dye molecules effectively attached to the surface. The electrons migrating to the surface react with water molecules, producing O2 ●
− radicals, which further reacted with the dye molecules and degraded them effectively (Fig. 7(b)). It should be noted that the dispersion or shift of VB and CB positions support their energy levels to be more suitable for the effective reduction and oxidation reactions essential for the photocatalytic degradation of the dye molecules [40].

To further investigate the underlying mechanism of the improved charge separation and transfer to the material surface, the photocurrent and electrochemical impedance measurements of the samples were carried out and the obtained results are displayed in Fig. 8 (a)-(b). The photocurrent density in both the extract-treated GCN samples exhibited an increase compared to pristine GCN. Similarly, the Nyquist plots, featuring decreased arc radii, indicate a significant reduction in the charge transfer resistance in extract-treated GCNs in comparison with the pristine system. These observations from photocurrent and impedance analyses suggest that both the intrinsic photocurrent conductivity and interfacial charge transfer characteristics are enhanced in the extract-treated GCN photocatalysts [51]. This may be originated due to the improved interactions between the exfoliated layers via the presence of functional groups and thereby to their interactions with surroundings leading to the rapid promotion of charge carriers to the surface. Furthermore, the defects may also serve as active centers to trap and sustain the excited electrons in the conduction band mitigating their rapid recombination with the holes, leading to the overall enhancements in their photocatalytic activities toward the effective degradation of the dye molecules.

The photocatalytic recyclability of V-GCN was explored through the degradation of mixed dyes over 5 consecutive cycles, and the results are displayed in Fig. 9 (a). It is evident that the degradation efficiency of V-GCN is consistent up to all 5 cycles with a minimal loss of around 2–3% during the 5th cycle. This confirms that the modifications induced in GCN due to the green treatment is stable and active, which collectively give rise to the overall chemical and photostability of the extract-treated photocatalysts. Further, the obtained XRD pattern, absorption spectrum, and FESEM image of the recycled V-GCN photocatalyst (Fig. 9(c)-(d)) substantiate the robust physiochemical characteristics of the system. This stability is intrinsic and integral part of the material, ensuring their persistent physiochemical properties for sustainable, durable and scalable photocatalytic applications.

It is worth considering that the properties of these plants and their extracts may vary depending on climatic conditions and geographical locations. As a result, the process by which these extracts influence the material modifications may also change. However, the fundamental phytochemical constituents of a given plant might largely remain consistent irrespective of locations. Thus, if these phytochemicals are carefully preserved in the extracts, the desired modifications in the materials can be effectively achieved, as demonstrated in this study.

Green-treatments over graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4/GCN) using the extracts of betel (Piper betle) leaves and veld grape (Cissus quadrangularis) stems were explored in this study. The results demonstrated that these green extracts effectively exfoliate GCN layers and modify both chemical and physical characteristics of GCN. Accordingly, the formation of functional groups, defects, and morphological changes in GCN, created through the extract-treatments, were confirmed by systemic characterization techniques such as XRD, XPS and FESEM. Further, the improved functionalization, surface area, and optical properties of the extract-treated GCN systems compare to pristine were characterized using FTIR, BET, UV-visible and PL spectroscopy techniques. The visible light-driven photocatalytic properties of the pristine and extract-treated GCN systems were investigated for the degradation of methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), and their mixture (MB+RhB). The enhanced photocatalytic efficiencies of the extract-treated GCN systems were attributed to the rapid generation of superoxide anion radicals, as confirmed by radical scavenging studies. The improved photocatalytic efficiencies were further linked to exfoliation- and nitrogen defects-mediated dispersion of energy bands in the system, leading to the rapid excitation, separation and transportation of charge carriers for the effective redox reactions. The findings of this study revealed that the green treatment has the potential to induce both physical and chemical modifications in g-C3N4 systems, which ultimately improved their physiochemical and photocatalytic properties.

R. Vijayarangan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sakar Mohan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Manickam Selvaraj: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Mohammed A. Assiri: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. R. Ilangovan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Group Research Project under grant number R.G.P. 2/354/44.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2023.103782.
